summary: Dan Shapiro explains the Incra positioning system for woodworking, demystifying how it differs from traditional dovetail jigs. He breaks down its three operating modes: micro-positioning for thousandth-of-an-inch adjustments, free sliding for large movements, and the key 'lock in' mode that snaps to the nearest 1/32 inch. The post argues that this snap-to-position mechanism enables perfect repeatability for dovetail joints. He concludes with a strong endorsement, noting that every woodworker he knows has adopted the system.
key_points: The Incra system is a router fence with a positioning system, not a traditional dovetail jig
The three operating modes (micro-positioning, free sliding, lock in) enable precise, repeatable cuts
The lock-in mode's snap-to-nearest-1/32-inch feature is the key innovation enabling perfect repeatability
The system comes with step-by-step templates that produce flawless joints even without full understanding of the mechanism
money_quotes: There's two things to understand about the Incra system. One is that it is a router fence, not a jig.

You can make a cut, slide it just about 1″ away, and it will snap to exactly 1″ away. Then you can move it back to your first cut, and it will snap to exactly where your first cut was. Over and over again, perfect repeatability.
They provide step-by-step instructions that will give you flawless joints every time, even if you don't understand how you got them.
Literally every woodworker I know uses an Incra now, many of them after trying mine out.
key_insight: The Incra positioning system's genius lies in its snap-to-position lock-in mode, which transforms imprecise manual movements into perfectly repeatable precision cuts.
